_meta.what_status_means: status reflects the content lead's editorial PUBLISH decision, not a pure voice-quality verdict. 'approved' = blessed as publishable / on-voice. 'not_approved' = NOT yet approved: a mix of drafts, pending review, and option-sets awaiting a pick. Do NOT treat 'not_approved' posts as off-voice negatives — most are fine, just unblessed.
_meta.the_real_preference_signal: Where a post has multiple options and one is approved (approved_option is set), that is a genuine pairwise preference — the strongest data here. Option-sets without a recorded pick are candidates for the preference game.
_meta.divij_version: Where present, divij_version is Divij's approved rewrite of Siddharth's draft. These are the strongest calibration signal in the dataset — same story, two voices, one approved. Use them as before/after anchors: Siddharth's options = what to avoid or improve; divij_version = the target register.
_meta.coverage_caveat: The approved set skews toward short design-post captions. The long-form 'founder sharing what we did' voice lives mostly in the Agency Updates divij_version rewrites. These are the primary anchors for long-form voice.

usage.positive_anchors: Use status=approved items and all divij_version entries as 'this is on-voice' few-shot anchors for the critic.
usage.false_positive_check: A correct critic should NOT flag approved items or divij_version entries as off-voice. Run the critic over these; any 'off-voice' verdict is a false positive to investigate.
usage.preference_accuracy: Within an option-set that has an approved_option, a correct comparative critic should prefer the approved option. This is the cleanest eval available in this file.
usage.do_not: Do not compute an 'agreement %' that counts not_approved posts as rejections — the labels do not support that.

options.text:
We've been working with @hobbes_ai and @chirag_sf for almost a year now.
It started with the product, then the website, then the demo agent interactions you see here.
That's what a long-term design partnership looks like in motion, literally.

options.text:
Our best lead this month came from someone we've never met.
A founder reached out this week, referred by a famous VC firm. We've never worked with them. Never pitched them. No relationship, no ask.
Someone inside saw our work and sent a founder their way.
He told us on the first call. Said he was told we were the only agency worth talking to for this.
We didn't see that coming. But whoever made that call, thank you.

examples.divij_version:
A founder booked a call this week, and partway through we realized we had no idea how he'd found us.

A ghost lead?

Turned out someone inside a well-known VC firm had seen our work and sent him our way. We'd never worked with them or even spoken to them.

What got us in the room was a sentence he'd been told before reaching out: we were the only agency worth talking to for this.

We didn't see it coming. Whoever made that call, thank you.
examples.divij_edit_notes: Opens on the moment of discovery, not the conclusion. 'A ghost lead?' is an honest reaction fragment (Rule 10 allowed). Drops 'Our best lead this month' — a summary that reveals the ending before the story starts (Rule 18).

options.text:
We won a new client this week.
They'd spoken to three other agencies before us. We didn't have the biggest portfolio in the room.
We had a point of view on their product and a date we'd commit to.
That's what closed it. Turned out most agencies gave them options and maybes. A real opinion and a real timeline is rarer than it should be.

examples.divij_version:
We had the smallest portfolio in the room. We still won the account.

The founder had already met three other agencies, all with more work in their industry to show than we had. On paper we were the weakest option.

What we brought instead was a clear point of view on his product and a date we'd commit to.

Everyone else had handed him options and maybe's. Nobody had just told him what they'd do and by when.

Turns out a real opinion and a real deadline are rarer than they should be.
examples.divij_edit_notes: Opens with the contradiction compressed (Rule 18). Buries the win and makes the reader earn it. 'On paper we were the weakest option' is more specific and honest than 'didn't have the biggest portfolio' (Rule 5). 'Nobody had just told him what they'd do and by when' adds a concrete detail that was missing entirely.

options.text:
We've never run a paid ad.
Our last three clients found us through a YC forum, a Slack community, and a VC referral.
We didn't plan it that way. That's just where the right founders are.

examples.media_note: (can attach a fake Slack ss here)
examples.divij_version:
People we've never met are sharing our website in rooms we'll never see.

We keep finding out after the fact. A founder books a call, and somewhere in it he mentions a YC forum thread, or a Slack channel we're not in, or a name who passed us along.

Our last three clients all came in this way, from places we didn't know were talking about us.

You can't buy that, and we didn't. The work just started traveling without us.
examples.divij_edit_notes: Hook pivots from a fact ('never run a paid ad') to the emotional truth behind it ('rooms we'll never see'). Rule 19: the most vivid detail moves to sentence one. 'The work just started traveling without us' is more evocative than 'that's just where the right founders are' — it names the feeling without stating the lesson (Rule 2).

options.label: Option 1
options.text:
Our clients kept asking if we do front-end dev.
Then motion. Then animation. Then product videos.
We kept saying no and referring them out. Last week we hired a design engineer.
We're not saying no anymore. When clients keep asking for the same thing, that's usually a sign.

options.label: Option 2
options.text:
We started as a design agency.
But our clients keep asking for more - front-end dev, motion, animation, product videos. And we just hired a design engineer to keep up.
It's a good sign when clients want more from you, and we are all for it.

examples.divij_version:
Our clients bullied us to hire a design engineer at Bricx.

They'd been asking for front-end dev, motion, animation, product videos. For months we said no and sent them elsewhere.

At some point the same request coming back over and over stops being a coincidence. So we hired for it.

We're not referring this work out anymore.
examples.divij_edit_notes: 'Bullied us' is a single unexpected but accurate word that does more work than Option 1's full setup paragraph (Rule 19 / Rule 6). Cuts 'When clients keep asking for the same thing, that's usually a sign' — that's telling the reader the lesson (Rule 2). 'We're not referring this work out anymore' closes with the fact, not an editorial.

options.label: Option 1
options.text:
Our project management isn't where it needs to be.
Both founders are still deep in day-to-day client work. Too much knowledge concentrated in two people.
The portfolio looks clean but the internal ops don't.
Fixing it this month.

options.label: Option 2
options.text:
Honest agency update.
Our project management isn't where it needs to be. Founders are managing individual client projects directly. Too much knowledge living in too few heads. It's causing stress and it's not sustainable.
We're growing - which is the good news. But growth that outruns your processes doesn't stay good for long.
This month we're fixing the infrastructure: clearer ownership, better handoffs, less founder dependency on day-to-day project decisions.
I'm sharing this because I think a lot of agencies hit this exact wall and nobody talks about it publicly. The portfolio looks clean. The chaos is internal.
If you've scaled through this, what changed everything for you operationally?

options.label: Option 3
options.text:
Honest update: our project management is breaking.
Me and @Divij are still running individual client projects ourselves. Too much living in too few heads.
We're growing faster than our processes can handle right now.
Uncomfortable to admit. Necessary to fix. Working on it this month

examples.divij_version:
Right now, @RakhraDivij and I are personally running client projects. That stopped being sustainable a while ago.

Too much of how this agency runs lives in two people's heads. The work itself is good. Keeping it good is taking more out of the two of us than it should.

We're growing, which is the reason this is happening. Growth has outrun the systems we built for a smaller version of us.

So the next few weeks go to fixing it. Clearer ownership on each project, and far less routed through the two of us.

This isn't the most comfortable update to post. But it's true. We're already on it.
examples.divij_edit_notes: Drops 'Honest agency update.' and 'Uncomfortable to admit. Necessary to fix.' — both are Rule 17 violations (labeling the emotion). Opens on the specific fact (@RakhraDivij, personally running projects) not the category ('project management'). 'Growth has outrun the systems we built for a smaller version of us' is more specific and real than 'growth that outruns your processes' (Rule 5). 'I'm sharing this because...' in Option 2 is preaching (Rule 2) — cut entirely.

options.label: Option 1
options.text:
A new Bricx Labs website is coming.
We've designed 50+ websites for AI and B2B SaaS startups.
Ours has been embarrassingly last on the list.
That changes this quarter, will share updates as we build.

options.label: Option 2
options.text:
We've designed 50+ websites for AI and B2B SaaS startups.
Somehow never got around to our own.
Starting this week. Sharing everything as we go.

options.label: Option 3
options.text:
We've redesigned websites for 50+ startups.
Somehow never got around to our own.
Starting this week. Sharing everything as we go.

examples.media_note: (a snippet of the WIP website or the existing Bricx website)
examples.divij_version:
The hardest website we've had to start is our own.

We've done 50+ for AI and B2B SaaS startups without blinking. Ours kept sliding down the list, partly because client work always won, partly because designing for yourself is its own kind of hard.

We're starting this week anyway. Updates as we build.
examples.divij_edit_notes: Opens with the contradiction compressed (Rule 18): 'hardest website... is our own' sets up the tension before explaining it. 'Without blinking' is a specific, honest feeling that contrasts with 'is its own kind of hard' (Rule 4). 'Ours kept sliding down the list' is more honest and specific than 'ours has been embarrassingly last' — which is a performance of self-deprecation.

options.label: Option 1
options.text:
Bricx Labs is being cited in Google's AI search answers now.
I didn't plan for it. No campaign, no agency, no strategy behind it.
The work just accumulated in enough places - case studies, client mentions, community threads, that it started being referenced automatically.
Best kind of distribution, completely free.

options.label: Option 2
options.text:
We spent $0 on Google ads.
Google started citing Bricx Labs in AI-generated search answers anyway.
No backlink campaign. No SEO agency. Nothing deliberate. Just enough case studies, client mentions and community threads that the algorithm started treating us as a reference.
Best distribution we never planned for.

examples.media_note: Flagged as not approved — citation not confirmed accurate for the time period. Factual issue, not a voice issue.

options.text: The $400M client that never converted

examples.media_note: Original was a reference only. Divij wrote the full post.
examples.divij_version:
A VP of Brand messaged us on WhatsApp five minutes after finding us. His company is valued at $3 billion.

He'd searched for the best UX agencies in Silicon Valley. We came up first. Next thing, he's on WhatsApp asking to hop on a call, and minutes later we're actually on it.

It didn't close. Doesn't matter. A company that size found us through a search ranking and reached out personally within the hour.

That's the whole pitch for AEO, in one slightly absurd afternoon.
examples.divij_edit_notes: Opens on two specific details in the first sentence: WhatsApp, five minutes (Rule 19). '$3 billion' comes immediately after — the number is the hook, not the explanation. 'In one slightly absurd afternoon' is real emotion tied to a real moment (Rule 4). Never states 'this proves AEO works' — lets the reader conclude it (Rule 2).

options.text:
Drop your website below.
I'll tell you the one thing above the fold that's costing you conversions.
Show me your hero section and I'll tell you if the value prop lands in 5 seconds or not.

Slow growth doesn't mean you need a new design.
It usually means your current one isn't saying the right thing.
We've saved founders $20-30k in redesign budgets just by fixing copy and information hierarchy first.
Redesign last. Always.

"Mobile-first" became a buzzword and everyone stopped doing it.
Most B2B SaaS products we look at are still desktop builds with a responsive stylesheet thrown on top.
Your users browse on their phone before they ever open a laptop.
That first impression is a design decision. Most teams aren't making it consciously.

Building a SaaS landing page in 2026:
Which hero converts better?

examples.media_note: (need to add 2 iterations of a LP)

options.text:
What actually triggers a startup redesign?
A) the CEO saw a competitor's site
B) growth stalled
C) a new funding round
D) the design team finally won the argument

options.label: Option 1
options.text:
half of design twitter has strong opinions and a weak portfolio.
having a take isn't the same as having taste.
the designers who can actually ship - inside real companies. with real constraints.real users. real pressure - are quieter online than you'd expect.

options.label: Option 2
options.text:
Half of design twitter has strong opinions and a weak portfolio.
Having a take isn't the same as having taste.
The designers who can actually ship - inside real companies, with real constraints, real users, real pressure, are quieter online than you'd expect.
Posting about design and doing design are two very different skills.

examples.divij_version:
Strong opinions, weak portfolio.

It's the most common combination in design twitter.

The correlation almost runs backwards. The sharper someone's hot takes, the thinner the body of work behind them tends to be. The ones who can actually do it are off doing it, not narrating it.

Posting about design and doing design are different skills, and the internet rewards the wrong one.
examples.divij_edit_notes: Hook compressed to 4 words vs a full sentence — contradiction stated in its sharpest form (Rule 18). 'The correlation almost runs backwards' is a specific, unexpected insight that neither option had. 'Off doing it, not narrating it' cuts the comma-triplet structure from Option 1 (Rule 11) and replaces it with a single precise observation.
